title: Orange You Glad It's Vitamin C Day?
story: Every April 4, people celebrate National Vitamin C Day by loading up on citrus fruits and healthy snacks! Plant orange trees in their backyard

title: Fancy French Food Gets Its Day
story: Chefs everywhere roll up their sleeves on April 4 for National Cordon Bleu Day! title: National Walk Around Things Day Gets Silly
story: Every April 4, people celebrate National Walk Around Things Day by doing exactly what it sounds like - walking around stuff instead of through it! Instead of cutting through the park, you stroll around its edges. Rather than walking through doorways normally, you might take the long way around furniture or trees. Some families make it a game, timing how long it takes to walk around their house, car, or even the grocery store. Social media fills with videos of people taking the scenic route around everything from puddles to playground equipment. It's a wonderfully weird way to slow down and notice your surroundings while getting extra steps in your day.

title: A New Name for a Historic School
story: Honoring a great leader, Ashmun Institute got a fresh start. On April 4, 1866, this school in Oxford, Pennsylvania changed its name to Lincoln University. The change celebrated President Abraham Lincoln, who had worked to end slavery. Lincoln University became an important place for African American students to learn and grow.
activity_question: What was the new name given to Ashmun Institute?
follow_up_question: Why do you think the school was renamed after President Lincoln?

title: A Car Guy's Grand Slam Idea
story: Batter up! In 1911, Hugh Chalmers stepped up to the plate with a game-changing idea. This car manufacturer pitched the concept of a Most Valuable Player award for baseball. On April 4, his suggestion sparked excitement, laying the groundwork for one of the sport's highest honors.
activity_question: What did Hugh Chalmers suggest for baseball?
activity_choices: A new type of baseball bat
follow_up_question: Why do you think a car manufacturer would be interested in baseball?

title: Lights, Camera, Warner Bros.!
story: Four brothers with big dreams changed movie history on April 4, 1923. Jack, Harry, Sam, and Albert Warner officially started Warner Bros. Pictures in sunny California. Their new company would go on to create some of the world's most beloved films and cartoons, bringing laughter and excitement to millions of people.
activity_question: Who started Warner Bros. Pictures?
follow_up_question: Can you name any famous Warner Bros. cartoons or characters?

title: The Scientist Who Found a Building Block
story: A curious boy named William Cumming Rose was born in Greenville, South Carolina in 1887. He grew up to become a brilliant biochemist who made an amazing discovery. On April 4, the same date as his birth, Rose uncovered threonine, a crucial amino acid. These tiny molecules are like Lego pieces that build proteins in our bodies!
activity_question: What did William Cumming Rose discover?
follow_up_question: Can you name another amino acid besides threonine?

title: A Star-Gazing Math Whiz Is Born
story: A brilliant mind joined the world on April 4, 1809. Benjamin Pierce, born in Salem, Massachusetts, would grow up to become a famous mathematician and astronomer. He created a special way to spot unusual data called Peirce's criterion. Pierce's love for numbers and stars helped unlock secrets of the universe!
activity_question: What special method did Benjamin Pierce create?
follow_up_question: Can you think of a time when spotting unusual data might be important?

title: A Presidential Baby Arrives
story: Tiny cries echoed through the Lincoln home in Springfield, Illinois. Abraham Lincoln's youngest son, Tad, had just been born! The year was 1853, and on April 4, the future president welcomed his fourth child. Tad grew up to be a lively, mischievous boy who brought joy and laughter to the White House during his father's presidency.
activity_question: Where was Tad Lincoln born?
bonus_fact: Tad wasn't his real name! He was nicknamed Tad because he wiggled like a tadpole as a baby.

title: Mary Colter: Architect of the Southwest
story: Blueprints and desert dreams filled Mary Colter's imagination. Born in Pittsburgh on April 4, 1869, she grew up to become a pioneering architect. Colter designed iconic buildings for the Fred Harvey Company and Santa Fe Railroad, shaping the look of the American Southwest. Her unique style blended local materials with Native American influences, creating unforgettable landmarks.
activity_question: What company did Mary Colter design buildings for?
follow_up_question: Can you name a famous building or landmark Mary Colter might have designed?
